subskills.name: Address-of operator (&) and why scanf needs it
Declaring typed pointers and common declaration pitfalls
Assigning addresses to pointers and dereferencing to read/write

questions.prompt: Why does scanf need the address-of operator (&)?
choices: To specify where the input value should be stored
To print the value of a variable
questions.correct_answer: To specify where the input value should be stored
rubric: The address-of operator (&) is used to pass the memory address of a variable to scanf so that the input value can be stored directly into that variable.
Without the address-of operator, scanf would not know where to store the input value.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly declares a pointer to an integer?
rubric: The asterisk (*) is used to declare a pointer variable.

questions.prompt: What does the following code do? int count = 5; int *countPtr = &count;
choices: It assigns the value of count to countPtr
It assigns the address of count to countPtr
It initializes countPtr with the value 5
It prints the value of count
questions.correct_answer: It assigns the address of count to countPtr
rubric: The address-of operator (&) is used to get the memory address of count.
This address is then assigned to the pointer variable countPtr.

questions.prompt: If p is a pointer to an integer, what does p++ do?
choices: Decrements the value of p by 1
Increments the value of p by 1
Increments the address stored in p by the size of an integer
Decrements the address stored in p by the size of an integer
questions.correct_answer: Increments the address stored in p by the size of an integer
rubric: For an integer pointer, p++ increments the address by the size of an integer (typically 4 bytes).
